symbols: dead body of Christ supported upright — the Man of Sorrows, displaying his suffering for contemplation
angels bearing the body — heaven receiving the sacrifice and presenting it for veneration
crown of thorns — the mockery of kingship turned into a crown of redemptive suffering
exposed wounds and bare chest — the full cost of atonement made visible
closed eyes — death endured, the price paid
description: A half-length composition showing the dead body of Christ, bare-chested with his head slightly tilted, eyes closed, and a crown of thorns upon his brow. Two young angels with sorrowful expressions support him from behind, one on each side. The figure of Christ is pale and muscular, bearing visible signs of suffering. A reddish-pink drapery is visible behind the angels. The background is dark and simple, focusing all attention on the intimate grouping of the dead Savior and his grieving angelic attendants.
theological_description: This image belongs to the devotional tradition of the Imago Pietatis (Image of Pity) — the dead Christ displayed for contemplation, supported by angels rather than lying in the tomb. The theological import is immense: this is God incarnate in death, the one 'through whom all things were made' (John 1:3) having submitted to the ultimate consequence of the Fall. Isaiah's prophecy is fulfilled: 'He was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5). The angels who attend him are the same heavenly beings who announced his birth with joy (Luke 2:13-14), now bearing his lifeless body in silent grief. Paul crystallizes this mystery: 'God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). The crown of thorns — the soldiers' mockery of his kingship (Matthew 27:29) — has become the true crown, for it is through suffering that Christ enters his glory. The deliberate display of the body invites the viewer into the mystery of the atonement: to behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29).

scripture_refs.reason: The Suffering Servant pierced and crushed for our sins — the dead Christ displays these wounds
Christ's death on the cross — 'It is finished' — the moment after which this scene occurs
God's love demonstrated through Christ's death while we were still sinners
The Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world — the body displayed for contemplation
The crown of thorns placed on Christ's head in mockery, visible in the image
